experiment.purpose: Locate the qeli client TUN-write throughput cliff for plain TCP vs full max-obfuscation TCP

root_cause_analysis.summary: Both cliffs are the SAME bug: client/mod.rs:182 spawns a fresh tokio::task::spawn_blocking(libc::write(tun)) for every inbound packet. Tokio's default blocking pool is 512 threads — under sustained high-pps traffic the pool overflows and the tunnel falls apart.
root_cause_analysis.why_max_obf_cliff_is_lower: Max-obf inflates packet rate at the same throughput: padding 256-byte average is added on top of every TCP segment, heartbeat fires 2 pps regardless of activity, fragmentation splits handshake records, traffic_normalization rounds 1380-byte segments up to the next 'natural' size (typically 1500). At 50 Mbps with 1380-byte segments that's already ~4.5k pps; max-obf doubles or triples that with extras. Plain TCP at 200 Mbps is ~18k pps, max-obf at 50 Mbps is ~12k+ pps — same effective pps zone hits the cliff.
evidence: Plain works cleanly at 10–200 Mbps with 0 retransmits, then hangs at 300 Mbps
Max-obf works cleanly at 10 Mbps (1 retr) and 50 Mbps (8 retr), then hangs at 100 Mbps
Both failure modes are identical: iperf3 'Connection timed out' after ~10 s, qeli client log shows TUN reader stopped, server log shows clean Client disconnected
root_cause_analysis.fix: Replace the per-packet spawn_blocking with one dedicated std::thread, same pattern used by server/mod.rs:373–407 (TUN writer). See ROADMAP.md P0.1.
root_cause_analysis.impact_on_REALITY: REALITY is INNOCENT. It does a peek-and-decide once per TCP accept (~hundreds of bytes), then hands the stream to the normal handler. It does NOT contribute to per-packet work. The 'TCP-max-obf with REALITY hangs' observation from earlier was misattributed — every isolated probe with just ONE obfuscation feature also hung, because each individual feature still bumps pps and we were testing uncapped iperf3 (effectively a stress test of the spawn_blocking cliff).
